coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots-modified reduced ultrathin g-C3N4 with strong photoredox capacity for broad spectrum-driven PPCPs remediation in natural water matrices

coredata.dc:description: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has been extensively applied for the photocatalytic degradation of pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs); however, its catalytic performance remains restricted by a poor photoresponse and modest photoredox capacity toward the generation of free radicals. Herein, a novel carbon quantum dots-modified reduced ultrathin g-C3N4 (RUCN/CQD) photocatalyst was successfully synthesized, with the capacity for direct •OH and O2 •− yields and a broad-spectrum response. Upon broad-spectrum light irradiation, the RUCN/CQD demonstrated a 100% removal rate for diclofenac (DCF) within six min. This remarkable performance was attributed to a customized band structure with a thermodynamic driving potential for the generation of •OH, CQD with an effective electron transfer capacity for promotional formation of O2 •−, and ultrathin porous structure with abundant reaction sites. Simultaneously, the up-converted fluorescent properties of the CQD endowed the photocatalyst with a broad-spectrum response. The RUCN/CQD system also exhibited superior photocatalytic activities under simulated natural conditions, which implied an immense potential for the remediation of PPCPs in ambient waterways.

(A) XRD spectra and (B) FT-IR spectra of UCN, RUCN, and RUCN/CQD; high-resolution XPS spectra of (C) C1s and (D) N1s of UCN, RUCN, and RUCN/CQD.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of BCN, UCN, RUCN, and RUCN/CQD; (B) room temperature EPR spectra of UCN, RUCN, and RUCN/CQD; (C) photoluminescence spectra, (D) normalized fluorescence decay, (E) transient photocurrent response and (F) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of BCN, UCN, RUCN, and RUCN/CQD.

(A) Potential transformation pathways of DCF during the RUCN/CQD photocatalytic degradation process; (B) schematic photocatalytic mechanism for the RUCN/CQD under broad-spectrum light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots-modified reduced ultrathin g-C3N4 with strong photoredox capacity for broad spectrum-driven PPCPs remediation in natural water matrices

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has been extensively applied for the photocatalytic degradation of pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs); however, its catalytic performance remains restricted by a poor photoresponse and modest photoredox capacity toward the generation of free radicals. Herein, a novel carbon quantum dots-modified reduced ultrathin g-C3N4 (RUCN/CQD) photocatalyst was successfully synthesized, with the capacity for direct •OH and O2 •− yields and a broad-spectrum response. Upon broad-spectrum light irradiation, the RUCN/CQD demonstrated a 100% removal rate for diclofenac (DCF) within six min. This remarkable performance was attributed to a customized band structure with a thermodynamic driving potential for the generation of •OH, CQD with an effective electron transfer capacity for promotional formation of O2 •−, and ultrathin porous structure with abundant reaction sites. Simultaneously, the up-converted fluorescent properties of the CQD endowed the photocatalyst with a broad-spectrum response. The RUCN/CQD system also exhibited superior photocatalytic activities under simulated natural conditions, which implied an immense potential for the remediation of PPCPs in ambient waterways.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) have been continually discharged into ambient water bodies through various anthropogenic sources [1,2]. More seriously, due to the low biodegradability of PPCPs, conventional wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) are (in many cases) unable to completely remove these compounds. The accumulation of PPCPs in natural aquatic environments pose a range of potentially adverse effects on human health and ecosystems [3–5]. Consequently, the development of efficient and environmentally-compatible strategies for the removal of recalcitrant PPCPs has emerged as a global challenge.

Toward the resolution of the energy crisis and environmental contamination, semiconductor photocatalysis technologies utilizing renewable solar energy has been recognized as an ideal strategy [6–8]. As a metal-free polymeric photocatalyst, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has garnered increasing attention due to its visible-light response, outstanding chemical stability, and environmental compatibility [9,10]. However, the bottleneck faced by g-C3N4 based photocatalysts in the area of environmental remediation relates to its insufficient oxidation potential. Its valence band (VB) potential at 1.57 eV (vs NHE) is inadequate for the direct oxidation of hydroxide (OH−) to hydroxyl radicals (•OH), which facilitate the degradation of organic contaminants [11]. To overcome this drawback, a common strategy is to combine g-C3N4 with other semiconductors to construct heterojunctions. And the heterojunction structure might endow composite materials with boosted redox capacities that result in the direct generation of •OH. Nevertheless, these strategies commonly involve poor stability and the leaching of harmful substances [12,13].

In our previous works, a facile self-modification strategy was employed to synthesize reduced graphitic carbon nitride (RCN) with both nitrogen vacancies (NVs) and cyano groups (C≡N) [14]. Following the simultaneous introduction of nitrogen vacancies and C≡N groups, the g-C3N4 charge density was redistributed via the generation of certain electron-rich domains. Such localized charge accumulation gave rise to the bandgap narrowing of RCN, which was accompanied by the positive shifts of conduction band (CB) and VB [15,16]. The band structure was modulated to induce effective optical absorption, while providing a sufficient driving force for the generation of •OH. Further investigations revealed that RCN exhibited augmentative photocatalytic performance for the degradation of PPCPs, where •OH played an essential role in the degradation process. Nevertheless, the practical application of g-C3N4 in natural water matrices remains restricted by its inherent disadvantages, including sluggish charge carrier kinetics for O2 reduction, insufficient long-wavelength visible and near-infrared light responses, as well as a low specific surface area.

As a novel carbon-based nanomaterial, carbon quantum dots (CQD) have gradually attracted the interest of researchers due to their low toxicity, easy synthesis, and inexpensive precursor. In addition, CQD possess qualities that are superior in terms of electrical conductivity and optical properties. As a result of these advantages, CQD have been extensively employed in photocatalysis [17,18]. Notably, CQD possess unique up-converted photoluminescence properties with the capacity to convert lower-energy light (visible and near-infrared light with wavelengths of from 500 ~ 900 nm) to high-energy light (ultraviolet and visible light with wavelength of from 325 ~ 425 nm), which leads to enhanced light response and absorption. Although rare earth doped nanoparticles (e.g., NaYF4:Yb, Er nanoparticles) also possess up-converted photoluminescence properties, these materials commonly involve complex synthesis steps and/or scarce raw materials [19,20]. Our previous studies also found that the introduction of CQD could effectively facilitate electron transfer, leading to the improved generation of O2 •− via the one-electron reduction of O2 on CQD surfaces [21–23]. Inspired by these concepts, we speculated that the combination of reduced graphitic carbon nitride and CQD might lead to an unexpected photoresponse and photoredox activity. Unfortunately, related reports remain extremely rare.

In this work, a novel CQD modified RUCN (RUCN/CQD) photocatalyst was fabricated via a two-step template-free thermal treatment method. The as-synthesized RUCN/CQD exhibited superior photocatalytic degradation activities for a representative PPCP (diclofenac, DCF) under visible light irradiation. The results of the ESR technique and trapping experiments revealed that O2 •− and •OH were primarily responsible for the degradation of the DCF. We also observed that the RUCN/CQD possessed favorable technical feasibility in practical aqueous media, including the treatment of DCF at various concentrations and mixing with natural water matrices and under solar light irradiation.

For this study, reduced ultrathin carbon nitride loaded with carbon quantum dots (RUCN/CQD) was synthesized via a two-step template-free thermal treatment method, which is illustrated in Fig. 1 A. Firstly, CQD was synthesized via a hydrothermal method according to our previous work [22], and ultrathin g-C3N4 (UCN) was prepared via a gas-template strategy with NH4Cl as the “gas releasing agent” [23]. Subsequently, the as-obtained UCN and different mass ratios of NaBH4 (0.0, 1.0, 3.0, 5.0, and 7.0 wt%) and different quantities of CQD (0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, and 0.4 wt%) were combined and finely ground with a mortar. The mixture was then calcined at 600 °C for 30 min under an N2 atmosphere at a ramping rate of 5 °C/min to obtain RUCN/CQD powders. The mixture was then calcined at 600 °C for 30 min under an N2 atmosphere at a ramping rate of 5 °C/min. Subsequently, the obtained RUCN/CQD powders were rinsed several times with ultrapure water and ethanol and dried at 75 °C overnight. For comparison, reduced ultrathin g-C3N4 (RUCN) was synthesized using the same process, except for the addition of CQD [16].

Photocatalytic performance experiments were conducted using a photoreactor (XPA-7, Nanjing XUJ Co. Ltd.) that was equipped with a 350 W Xe lamp and a 420 nm filter, which provided visible light irradiation. The experiment control reaction temperature was maintained at 25 °C, at a speed of 900 r/min. Moreover, a monochromatic light lamp with wavelengths of 365 nm, 450 nm, 520 nm, 660 nm, and 750 nm was employed to emanate UV, blue, green, red, and near-infrared light, respectively, to investigate the photocatalytic performance of the photocatalyst under broad-spectrum light. During each photocatalytic experiment, 25 mg of the sample was dispersed within an aqueous solution of DCF (25 mL, 5 mg/L) in a quartz tube.

The suspension was ultrasonicated for 5 min and stirred for 20 min in the dark to attain adsorption–desorption equilibrium prior to irradiation. During irradiation, 1.0 mL aliquots of the suspension were periodically extracted and filtered (0.22 μm Millipore filter) to remove the powder prior to analysis. The various concentrations of residual DCF were analyzed using high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC-20AT, SHIMADZU). The photocatalytic degradation rate (η) of DCF was calculated using the following equation:

The photocatalytic intermediates of DCF were determined using Thermo Scientific Ultimate 3000 RSLC high resolution liquid chromatography, coupled with Q Exactive Orbitrap accurate mass spectrometry (HRAM LC-MS/MS).

As illustrated in Fig. 1A, ultrathin carbon nitride (UCN) and CQD were synthesized using gas template and hydrothermal methods, respectively. Unlike the bulk g-C3N4 (BCN) with a compact layered-like structure, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images revealed that the UCN exhibited a porous and crinkly nanosheet structure, which was caused by the gas released from the NH4Cl during heating (Figs. S1A, B and Fig. 1B, E). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) revealed the rounded shapes of the CQD, with an average diameter of ~ 5 nm (Fig. S1C). Finally, a mixture of CQD and NaBH4 was ground with the UCN, which was then calcined at 600 °C to obtain the RUCN/CQD composites. Subsequent to these processes, the CQD were randomly deposited on the RUCN surface. As illustrated in Fig. 1C and F, following the NaBH4 treatment, the morphology of the RUCN changed negligibly in contrast to the UCN, except for a small increase in irregular pores. The HRTEM images in the Fig. 1G inset demonstrated that the CQD were successfully anchored on the RUCN. Furthermore, the inset revealed that the CQD had obvious lattice fringes with d-spacings of 0.21 nm, which corresponded to the lattice planes of graphite [24].

Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping shown in Figs. S1E-G demonstrated that C, N, and O were homogeneously distributed across the sample. As illustrated in Fig. S2A and Table S1, the specific surface area (SBET) and the pore volume of the RUCN were calculated to be 61.7 m2/g and 0.355 cm3/g, respectively, which were obviously higher than that of the BCN (16.3 m2/g and 0.116 m2/g). Such an enhanced SBET and pore volume might provide additional reactive sites that enhance photocatalytic activity. Following the introduction of the CQD, the SBET and the pore volume of the RUCN/CQD were decreased to 48.2 m2/g and 0.293 cm3/g, respectively. These reductions might have been due to the spatial occupations of the CQD.

The crystal structures and chemical features of the prepared nanosheet samples (UCN, RUCN, and RUCN/CQD) were investigated via powder x-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns and FT-IR spectra. As shown in the XRD pattern (Fig. 2 A), all of samples revealed two diffraction peaks at 2θ = 12.9° and 27.5°, which represent the in-plane structural packing of tri-s-triazine units ((100) planes) and interlayer stacking of aromatic structures ((002) planes), respectively [25]. However, compared with the UCN, the peak intensity of (100) and (002) in RUCN and RUCN/CQD were significantly weakened, which indicated the loss of ordered structures within the g-C3N4 framework caused by the NaBH4 treatment [16]. The crystal structure of RUCN was negligibly altered subsequent to coupling with the CQD, which might have been due to the relatively low CQD content [26].

In addition, the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the samples (Fig. 2B) had a typical absorption centered at 810 cm−1, 1200–1700 cm−1, and 3000–3700 cm−1, which corresponded to the bending mode of triazine units, stretching vibration modes of C–N heterocycles, and N–H stretching vibrations, respectively [27]. Remarkably, both the RUCN and RUCN/CQD exhibited a decrease in the peak intensity at 810 cm−1, and a new peak emerged at 2180 cm−1, which belonged to C≡N groups [28]. This phenomenon indicated that the formation of C≡N groups originated from the opening of s-triazine subunits [15]. Following the loading of CQD, a slight absorption band at 2359 cm−1 (C≡C) corresponding to the π-π* transition photoluminescence of the CQD could be observed [29]. Therefore, the above results indicated that chemical interactions were initiated between the RUCN and CQD.

To gain further insights into the molecular structures of these samples, which incorporated nitrogen vacancies and C≡N groups, solid-state 13C cross-polarization magic angle spinning nuclear magnetic resonance (CP-MAS-NMR) measurements were conducted. It could be seen that the NMR spectra of both the UCN and RUCN (Fig. S2C) showed two strong peaks at 164.7 and 156.9 ppm, which corresponded to characteristic atomic C (1) of CN3 and C (2) of CN2 (NHx) in the heptazine units, respectively [30]. Compared with the UCN, the peak intensities of C (1) and C (2) for the RUCN exhibited a lower intensity, as well as a small split presented in the C (1) peak, which might have been attributed to the presence of nitrogen vacancies [31]. Of note is that two new peaks at 122.4 and 171.8 ppm appeared in the RUCN, which were assigned to the C (4) atoms of C≡N groups and their neighboring C (3) atoms, respectively [32]. Therefore, the simultaneous modification of nitrogen vacancies and C≡N groups were achieved in the g-C3N4 framework.

The chemical compositions and binding states of the samples were further examined by x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The contents (at%) of C, N, and the surface atom ratios (C/N) for the samples are listed in Table S2. A careful examination revealed that the C/N ratio of the RUCN (1.92) was higher than that of the UCN (1.60), which suggested the introduction of nitrogen vacancies [33]. Moreover, due to the presence of the CQD, the RUCN/CQD C/N ratio was further increased to 2.36. The high-resolution O 1 s spectrum of the samples (Fig. S2D and Table S3) exhibited two obvious peaks with binding energies of 531.9 eV and 533.4 eV, which were ascribed to the chemisorbed oxygen of surface hydroxyls (C-OH) and the 1 s–p* transition in the epoxide structure (C = O), respectively [23]. The C = O intensity was increased from 40.9% to 42.5% subsequent to the introduction of CQD due to its resident carboxyl groups.

The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2C and Table S4) could be divided into three peaks centered at 284.8, 286.1, and 288.1 eV, which corresponded to C–C, C–OH, and N–C = N, respectively [34]. The enhancement of the peak intensity of C-OH was ascribed to the water adsorbed to the surfaces of the samples, and the oxygen-containing functional groups of the CQD. The N 1 s spectra (Fig. 2D and Table S5) could be deconvolved into four peaks at 398.3, 399.1, 400.5, and 404.1 eV, which were assigned to a sp2-hybridized N atom attached to C in the aromatic ring (N2C), bridging N atoms in N-(C)3 (N3C), amino functional groups (C–NH), and graphitic species, respectively [35]. According to our previous studies and the above results, the percentage of N(sp2) groups was reduced compared with the UCN, which illustrated that nitrogen vacancies were formed in the position of N2C. In conclusion, the potential structural changes in the heptazine units of the UCN, as induced by the NaBH4 thermal treatment is proposed in Fig. 1A, with C≡N groups introduced at one apex of the melon structure and nitrogen vacancies stemming from the decomposition of the N = C–N units.

The optical absorption of the photocatalysts was investigated by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS). As shown in Fig. 3 A, the UCN exhibited an absorption edge at 460 nm. In contrast to the UCN, the absorption edge of the RUCN exhibited an obvious red shift due to the existence of nitrogen defects. Significantly, following the introduction of the CQD, the absorption edge red-shifted to 480 nm and showed intense absorption across UV to NIR regions, which indicated that the CQD could efficiently broaden the spectral response.

The optical properties of the CQD were further investigated via photoluminescence (PL) spectra. Fig. S3 reveals that the CQD could emit short wavelength light (350 ~ 600 nm) when they were excited by long wavelength light (600 ~ 900 nm). Furthermore, the band gap energies (Eg) of the UCN and RUCN were calculated to be 2.75 and 2.64 eV, respectively, according to the Tauc approach (Fig. S4A) [36]. The narrower Eg indicated a higher visible light harvesting capability, which produced additional photoinduced charge carriers under visible light irradiation.

Mott–Schottky (MS) tests were conducted to further determine the band structure of the samples. As depicted in Figs. S4B and C, the flat-band potentials of UCN and RUCN were determined to be −1.10 and −0.75 V (vs the Hg/Hg2Cl2 electrode), respectively, which corresponded to −0.86 and −0.51 V (vs NHE) [37]. The positive slopes of the fitted liner plots demonstrated that all of the samples were typical n-type semiconductors with conduction band (CB) positions located close to the flat-band potential. Therefore, the CB of the UCN and RUCN were estimated to be −0.86 and −0.51 eV, respectively. Combined with the Eg result, the valence band (VB) positions of the UCN and RUCN were calculated to be 1.89 and 2.13 eV, respectively, with the detailed band structure scheme depicted in Fig. S4D. The bandgap narrowing of RUCN was accompanied with positive shifts of the CB and VB, which was due to the simultaneous modification of nitrogen vacancies and C≡N groups. Due to the low Fermi level (Ef) of CQD (0 eV vs NHE), the photogenerated electrons in RUCN easily transferred to CQD, which resulted in the efficient separation of electrons and holes [38].

The electronic structures of the photocatalysts were revealed through room temperature electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra (Fig. 3B). A single Lorentzian line with a g value of 2.0034 was observed for the samples, which indicated the presence of unpaired electrons in the C atoms of the heptazine rings [39]. In contrast to the UCN, this EPR signal was strengthened in the RUCN, which we deduced to be related to the modification of nitrogen defects, which opened the heptazine units. This provided additional unsaturated sites that facilitated the formation of unpaired electrons. Moreover, the EPR intensity of the RUCN/CQD was further amplified due to the π-π conjugation between the RUCN and CQD, which enabled the photogeneration of charge carriers [17].

To assess the radiative recombination processes of photogenerated charge carriers in the samples, the PL spectra were measured. As presented in Fig. 3C, the UCN and RUCN possessed lower PL intensities compared with the BCN, which suggested a lower recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. This might be explained by the ultrathin structure and nitrogen defects, which promoted the separation efficiency of charge carriers. Meanwhile, the PL intensity of the RUCN/CQD was further reduced due to the charge transfer functions of the CQD.

Subsequently, time-resolved PL spectra (Fig. 3D and Table S6) were employed to investigate the lifetime of photoexcited charge carriers. According to the calculated results, the average lifetime of the as-prepared samples gradually decreased from 4.062 ns (BCN) to 1.677 ns (RUCN/CQD). The shortened lifetime demonstrated that efficient electron transfer occurred in the RUCN/CQD [40]. The transient photocurrent responses and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of the samples were studied further to elucidate their charge transfer efficiencies. As anticipated, the RUCN/CQD exhibited a stronger photocurrent intensity and a shorter Nyquist curve diameter between all of the photocatalyst materials (Fig. 3E, F). This enhancement signified that the RUCN/CQD possessed a higher transfer efficiency of photoinduced charges and electronic conductivity.

According to the above investigation, the customized band structure of the RUCN/CQD was observed to increase the oxidizing driving-force, while the anchored CQD served as electron acceptors for effective charge transfer. Further, due to the up-conversion attributes of the CQD, they could converse and transmit photonic energy to enhance optical absorbance, spanning the visible to NIR frequencies. Thus, the combination of band structure engineering with the introduction of CQD might significantly optimize the photocatalytic activity of the RUCN/CQD.

The degradation of various PPCPs (including diclofenac, naproxen, triclosan, and bisphenol A) under visible light irradiation was undertaken to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of the RUCN/CQD catalyst. As shown in Fig. S5, the RUCN/CQD exhibited remarkable photocatalytic activity for the degradation of all PPCPs. Specifically, the removal rate of diclofenac, naproxen, triclosan, and bisphenol A were calculated to be 93.8%, 83.7%, 71.7%, and 47.0%, respectively. This indicated that the photocatalytic process involved in RUCN/CQD possessed favorable universality for the photocatalytic degradation of various PPCPs.

To further study the photocatalytic mechanisms of RUCN/CQD, we selected diclofenac (DCF) as a model contaminant, as it had exhibited the highest photodegradation activity. Fig. S6A revealed that the adsorption–desorption equilibrium could be obtained within 20 min in the dark. Compared with the BCN, the RUCN/CQD with ultrathin porous structures showed a higher removal rate for DCF, which was the result of its larger specific surface area. As shown in Fig. 4 A, without any photocatalyst the degradation of DCF was negligible after 40 min of visible light irradiation, indicating the high photostability of DCF to visible light. Only 9.1% of the DCF was degraded in the presence of the BCN, while 30.5% and 59.2% degradation occurred with the UCN and RUCN, respectively, under the same conditions. Evidently, the photocatalytic activity was effectively improved following the morphological control and NaBH4 treatment. Subsequent to the introduction of CQD, the degradation rate of the DCF achieved by the RUCN/CQD (0.2 wt%) was 93.8%, which suggested that the CQD were critical for augmenting the photocatalytic performance of the RUCN.

The effects of the addition of NaBH4 and CQD on photocatalytic degradation of DCF were further investigated under visible light exposure. Furthermore, the DCF degradation process conformed to the pseudo first-order reaction kinetics model. As shown in Fig. S6B, when the quantity of NaBH4 was increased from 0.0 to 3.0 wt%, the kinetic rate constant (Kobs) increased from 0.007 to 0.0204 min−1, which was attributed to the introduction of nitrogen vacancies and C≡N groups generated by the NaBH4 treatment. The appropriate populations of nitrogen vacancies and C≡N groups could augment the photoinduced charge separation and oxidation performance of the g-C3N4, leading to an enhancement of its photocatalytic activity.

Nevertheless, further increases in the quantity of NaBH4 would cause excessive nitrogen vacancies to disproportionately trap photoinduced charges, thereby increasing the recombination of charge carriers. Therefore, the RUCN with 3.0 wt% NaBH4 doping was the optimal sample, which was selected to further study the effects of CQD loading (Fig. 4B). The Kobs of the RUCN/CQD was calculated to be 0.0681 min−1 when the CQD loading amount was increased to 0.2 wt%, which was 34.1, 9.7, and 3.3 times that of the BCN, UCN, and RUCN, respectively. This superior photocatalytic activity was the result of the excellent optical properties and strong charge transfer capacities of the CQD. However, the DCF degradation efficiency was reduced with higher CQD loadings, which may have been ascribed to the optical shielding effect of the CQD. To further investigate the degradation kinetics of the RUCN/CQD, degradation experiments were carried out under various catalyst concentrations and light sources.

Initially, the impacts of the photocatalyst dosage on the removal of DCF by RUCN/CQD was investigated in the range of from 0.4 ~ 1.5 g/L. As shown in Figs. S6C, 4C, the removal rate of DCF was obviously enhanced when the photocatalyst concentration was increased from 0.4 to 1.0 g/L under 40 min of visible light irradiation. However, only a slight enhancement in the DCF degradation rate was observed with further increases of photocatalyst. This might have been the influence of particle aggregation and light scattering caused by the presence of excess photocatalysts. Consequently, 1.0 g/L was selected as the optimal photocatalyst concentration based on the balance of a relatively high degradation rate and low cost photocatalyst.

Moreover, to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of the RUCN/CQD under different light sources, the degradation of DCF was investigated under various single-wavelength light exposures (e.g., 365 nm, 450 nm, 520 nm, 660 nm, and 750 nm). As depicted in Fig. 4D, the RUCN/CQD exhibited a higher photocatalytic performance under all tested light exposures, which demonstrated that their photocatalytic activity was 1.53, 2.51, 4.97, 5.26, and 8.36 times than that of the RUCN under wavelengths of 365 nm, 450 nm, 520 nm, 660 nm, and 750 nm, respectively. This enhancement was attributed to the excellent up-converted fluorescent properties and favorable charge transfer capacities of the CQD, which yielded efficient photocatalysts with a broad-spectrum response.

To gain insights into the reactive species (RSs) involved in the process of DCF photodegradation, the electron spin resonance (ESR) technique using DMPO as spin-trapping agent was performed. As illustrated in Fig. 5 A, B, no ESR signals could be observed in the dark. The characteristic peaks for DMPO-O2 •− and DMPO-•OH adducts were detected under visible light irradiation, which indicated the presence of O2 •− and •OH in both the UCN and RUCN/CQD photocatalytic systems.

However, the signal intensities of both DMPO-O2 •− and DMPO-•OH in the RUCN/CQD were stronger than those of the UCN, which suggested that the NaBH4 treatment and CQD loading could boost the generation of O2 •− and •OH, thereby improving photocatalytic performance. In addition, as shown in Fig. S6D, the DMPO-O2 •− intensity in the RUCN/CQD was obviously higher than that of the RUCN. This was due to the presence of the CQD, which facilitated electron transfer and resulted in a stronger O2 reduction capacity. Notably, under a nitrogen atmosphere (Fig. 5C), the UCN system exhibited only a negligible •OH signal, which indicated that the UCN could not directly oxidize OH− to •OH (OH−/•OH = 1.99 eV) due to its inherent VB position (1.89 eV) [34]. In contrast, the RUCN/CQD system exhibited an obvious signal under the same conditions. This confirmed that the RUCN/CQD sample was indeed a sufficiently oxidized driving-force for the direct generation of •OH, which was attributed to the modification of the VB position, from 1.89 eV to 2.13 eV.

Further, RSs trapping experiments were performed to quantitatively analyze the roles of various RSs during the photocatalytic process (Fig. 5D, Table S7). p-benzoquinone (PBQ), Isopropanol (IPA), and sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4) were employed as scavengers of superoxide radicals (O2 •−), hydroxyl radicals (•OH), and photogenerated holes (h+), respectively [41]. Following the addition of PBQ, the degradation efficiency of DCF was significantly decreased, with an inhibition rate of 86.5%. Further, the degradation efficiencies of DCF under the IPA and Na2C2O4 scavengers were decreased by 56.7% and 51.9%, respectively. This indicated that O2 •− was the major active substance, while •OH and h+ exerted secondary effects toward the photocatalytic degradation of DCF.

The degradation intermediates of DCF were investigated by HRAM LC-MS/MS analysis, where ten possible transformation products (P1-P10) were successfully identified in positive ion mode. Their tentative structures and mass spectra analysis are shown in Table S8 and Fig. 6A. The degree of DCF mineralization in the RUCN/CQD photocatalytic process was evaluated by TOC removal efficiencies. As shown in Fig. S7A, a TOC removal efficiency of 51.4% was achieved under visible light irradiation for 90 min, which revealed that the DCF and its photocatalytic degradation intermediates could be completely mineralized.

Combined with above results, three primary degradation pathways could be classified as hydroxylation, photocyclization, and C-N cleaving. Firstly, the DCF molecule was attacked by •OH, leading to the formation of P1 (m/z = 312.0181), which could be further oxidized into a corresponding quinone imine intermediate P2 (m/z = 310.0025) [26]. Subsequently, the quinone imine derivative degradation step involved the decarboxylation of the phenylacetic acid group to P3 (m/z = 266.1645) and the further hydroxylation of the aliphatic chain of P3, which led to the formation of P4 (m/z = 282.2033) [42].

Secondly, photocyclization might occur in the DCF to form P5 (m/z = 259.1534), which originated from an e- attack [43]. The P5 yielded a new five-membered ring between chlorobenzene and toluene, which resulted from the loss of chlorine and hydrogen atoms. Following the photocyclization reaction on the photocatalyst surfaces, P5 was further oxidized by O2 •− to generate P6 (m/z = 230.2474) [44].

Thirdly, the oxidation byproduct P7 (m/z = 162.9687) was generated via C-N cleavage and subsequent deethylation [45]. Subsequently, the relevant intermediates were further attacked, leading to the generation of P8 (m/z = 128.0696), P9 (m/z = 151.1115) and P10 (m/z = 166.9883). Finally, the intermediates could be further mineralized to CO2 and H2O.

Furthermore, we found that the DCF degradation pathways above were similar with our previous results in a RCN system [14]. For example, both of the above I and II pathways, and pathways II and III in the RCN system were initiated by hydroxylation and photocyclization reactions, respectively. However, additional small molecular weight products were detected during the degradation process in the RUCN/CQD system. This might have been due to the stronger redox capacity of the RUCN/CQD system, which was favorable for the generation of reactive species; thus, exhibiting an improved DCF degradation efficiency.

According to the above results, the mechanism of the photocatalytic RUCN/CQD system was proposed, as shown in Fig. 6 B. Under broad-spectrum light irradiation, RUCN could directly absorb short-wavelength UV and visible light (<470 nm) to generate electron-hole pairs. Meanwhile, other visible and NIR light with long wavelengths could be transformed by the up-converted attributes of the CQD to short-wavelength light, which then excited the photocatalyst. Of benefit to the enhanced charge separation rate resulting from the electron acceptor function of the CQD and the charge trapping sites of nitrogen defects, photogenerated charge carriers efficiently migrated to the surface of the photocatalyst.

Subsequently, photogenerated electrons could readily capture O2 to form O2 •− as the CB potential of the RUCN (-0.51 eV) was more negative than the redox potential of O2/O2 •− (-0.33 eV). On the other hand, the simultaneous modification of nitrogen vacancies and C≡N groups modulated the VB position of the RUCN to 2.13 eV, which endowed it with a higher oxidation capacity to facilitate the direct decomposition of contaminants or react with OH− to produce •OH. Simultaneously, organic contaminants were efficiently adsorbed and enriched on the extensive surface areas of the ultrathin porous structures. Finally, the adsorbed contaminants could be attacked by various RSs (O2 •−, •OH, h+), leading to their degradation and mineralization.

The long-term activity of catalysts is a critical property when they are employed for practical applications. Here, the sustainability of the RUCN/CQD was evaluated via cycling experiments. As shown in Fig. 7 A, the photocatalytic performance for DCF degradation was > 90% even after six successive cycles. Furthermore, the XRD diffraction pattens and FT-IR spectra of the cycled samples (Figs. S8A, B) were similar to that recorded before the reaction. These results indicated that the RUCN/CQD had favorable sustainability, which translated to its potential value in practical applications.

In real world wastewater treatment processes, initial PPCP concentrations are likely to be variable; thus, we conducted degradation experiments under different DCF concentrations to test the universality of the RUCN/CQD system. Fig. 7B demonstrates that 1 mg/L of DCF could be completely decomposed within 20 min. When the concentration of DCF was increased 15.0 mg/L, the removal efficiency still achieved at 64.2% within 40 min. Therefore, the RUCN/CQD system might be universally applied for the treatment of various concentrations of wastewater contaminants.

To evaluate the technical feasibility of the RUCN/CQD photocatalytic system, experiments were conducted to degrade DCF in various ambient water matrices. As shown in Fig. 7C, compared with the DCF removal efficiency in DI water (93.8%), removal efficiencies were decreased by 9.8%, 11.7%, 9.5%, 12.9%, and 5.4% in tap water, WWTP effluent, Pearl River water, lake water, and South China sea water, respectively. This inhibition might have been attributed to the presence of organic matter and various ions in ambient water, which might serve as light filters and electron trappers.

The interference of natural water constituents on the degradation of DCF was further investigated. Fig. 7D reveals that Cl−, SO4 2−, and NO3 − affected the photocatalytic degradation activities of DCF only negligibly, while it suffered from moderate suppression in the presence of HCO3 −. This is because HCO3 − could quench •OH to form less reactive •CO3 − at a secondary rate constant of 8.5 × 106 M−1 S-1 [46]. Moreover, following the addition of cations (Cu2+ and Fe3+), the degradation of DCF was obviously inhibited. In the presence of Cu2+ and Fe3+, the reactions of O2 •− with Cu2+ and Fe3+ were conducted prior to the degradation of DCF due to the high secondary rate constant (

=2.4 × 106 M−1 S-1) [47,48]. Similarly, humic acid (HA) exhibited a significant inhibitory effect on the degradation of DCF, which was attributed to the fact that HA could serve, not only as a RSs (•OH and O2 •−) scavenger but compete for photon absorption [49].

As an important parameter of the water samples, pH might affect the protonation-deprotonation reaction of organic compounds and the surface charges of photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 7E, an increase in pH (from 3 to 11) might have negative impacts on the degradation of DCF and the reason is worth revealing. The dissociation constant (pKa) of DCF was 4.35, which suggested that it presented protonation species at pH < 4.35, while it presented deprotonation species at pH > 4.35 [50]. Furthermore, Fig. S7D reveals that the RUCN/CQD possessed a negative polarity on its surface at pH ≥ 3. Therefore, electrostatic attraction between the RUCN/CQD and protonated DCF might occur when the pH of the system is < 4.35, thereby enhancing the activity of the RUCN/CQD system. Conversely, electrostatic repulsion might have appeared between the RUCN/CQD and deprotonated DCF when the pH exceeded 4.35, which inhibited its degradation.

To further assess the practical applicability of the photocatalytic RUCN/CQD process to degrade PPCPs in practical applications, we conducted DCF degradation experiments using natural sunlight as the light source (Fig. 7F). Surprisingly, all of the DCF molecules were completely degraded after six min of natural sunlight exposure, which implied that natural sunlight could serve as the ideal energy source for the RUCN/CQD system for the eradication of PPCPs. Notably, the photocatalytic performance of the natural sunlight driven RUCN/CQD system was far superior than other photocatalytic systems (Table S9) [44,51–55]. In summary, the superior photocatalytic performance of the metal-free RUCN/CQD system in simulated natural conditions enabled its strong potential for practical wastewater treatment applications.

In conclusion, we successfully synthesized a novel metal-free RUCN/CQD photocatalyst via a facile two-step thermal treatment method. The ultrathin porous structure provided abundant reactive sites. The band structure was significantly tuned through the simultaneous introduction of nitrogen vacancies and C≡N groups into g-C3N4, which gave rise to both effective optical absorption and higher oxidizing capacity to directly generate •OH. Besides, the electron acceptor function of the CQD facilitated the reduction of O2, which involved the one-electron pathway. Combined with the PL up-conversion properties of the CQD, the RUCN/CQD exhibited outstanding photocatalytic activity for the degradation of DCF, which was 34.1, 9.7, and 3.3 times that of the BCN, UCN, and RUCN, respectively. Moreover, the RUCN/CQD possessed favorable sustainability, while efficiently treating PPCPs in natural water matrices and under natural sunlight irradiation. This study provided not only a sustainable and environmentally compatible strategy for the treatment of PPCPs contaminated water, but shed new light on the design of g-C3N4-based photocatalysts with powerful photoredox capacities under broad-spectrum light irradiation.
